author: Sofia Martínez
title: Multi-Location SEO: Dominate Search Across All Your Business Locations
description: Stop cannibalizing your own rankings. Master Google Business Profile strategy so each location ranks independently and drives local traffic.

sections.p: You've got 4 locations. You search "plumber near me" in Location A, and your Location B shows up instead. Or worse, only 1 of your 3 locations appears in the Local Pack. This is the multi-location SEO trap: without proper structure, your locations compete with each other for visibility instead of working together to dominate the market. Franchise and chain businesses lose 30–50% of potential leads because their SEO strategy treats all locations like one big mess.

sections.h2: Why Your Locations Are Cannibalizing Each Other

sections.p: Google doesn't see your business as "Smith HVAC" with 4 branches. It sees 4 separate entities. If they all have the same phone number, the same exact business description, and point to the same homepage, Google gets confused about which one is authoritative. When someone searches "HVAC repair" in your city, Google has to pick: show all four, show one, or show none and let a competitor take the spot. Most of the time, only 1–2 locations rank, and they're usually the ones with the most reviews. Your newest location—the one that needs customers most—stays invisible.

list: All locations share one phone number or website
Google Business Profiles are identical (same description, hours, photos)
Your website doesn't have unique pages for each location
No location-specific content ("Plumber in Brooklyn" vs. "Plumber in Queens")
You haven't set up location extensions in Google Ads properly

sections.p: Here's what wins: each location gets its own everything. Own Google Business Profile. Own local phone number (not a vanity line). Own website landing page. Own citation presence in local directories. But they all connect back to your main brand.

Start with your Google Business Profiles. If you have 5 locations, you need 5 separate verified profiles—all under one Google Business Account (the parent account). This way, Google understands they're part of the same organization but distinct entities. Each profile gets its own local phone number. Not a shared 800 number. A real 718 number for your Brooklyn location, a real 516 number for your Long Island location. This alone improves local search visibility by 25–40% because Google can now match local intent to local listings.

sections.h2: Build Location Pages That Rank Independently

sections.p: Your website needs a dedicated page for each location. Not a dropdown menu. Not a sidebar. A full page: yoursite.com/plumber-brooklyn, yoursite.com/plumber-queens. On the Brooklyn page: put the phrase "plumber in Brooklyn" in the title tag, H1, first paragraph, and schema markup. Not keyword stuffing—it should read naturally. "Our Brooklyn plumbing team serves Kings County with emergency service 24/7." Include a unique photo of your Brooklyn team or the storefront. Add the Brooklyn location's phone number. Embed the Google Map with a pinned marker at that exact address.

list: Title tag: "Plumber in Brooklyn | [Brand Name] — 24/7 Emergency Service"
Location-specific content: mention nearby neighborhoods, streets, or landmarks
Unique photos: team, storefront, or job site from that location
Schema markup: LocalBusiness with address, phone, opening hours specific to that location
Internal links: link from this page back to your service pages and main site

sections.quote: We restructured a 6-location HVAC company's website. Within 90 days, 4 locations that weren't ranking jumped into the Local Pack. They went from 8 leads per month across all locations to 35.

sections.h2: Citations: The Unsexy Foundation That Moves the Needle

sections.p: Citations are business listings in directories: Yelp, Yellow Pages, Angie's List, industry-specific directories. For a plumber, it's Yelp, HomeAdvisor, and local chamber sites. For a dentist, it's Healthgrades and Zocdoc. For a car repair shop, it's Yelp and the Better Business Bureau. Each location needs consistent citations with its own local address and phone number.

This takes time, but it matters. A study by Bright Local found that businesses with citations in 5+ local directories get 25% more clicks from local search results. Build a spreadsheet: columns are directories, rows are locations. Track where each location is listed. Aim for at least 20 consistent citations per location. Use a tool like BrightLocal or Semrush's local SEO tool to auto-populate citations at scale—saves 15 hours of manual work.

sections.h2: The Technical Setup That Locks It In

sections.p: Schema markup tells Google what information belongs where. Use LocalBusiness schema for each location page. Include: address, phone number, opening hours, service area (if relevant), and a link to the parent brand. Most WordPress sites can do this with Yoast SEO or RankMath plugins. Custom sites need a developer to add schema to the page header. This takes 1–2 hours per location, but it eliminates confusion about which location serves which area.
